content:
# Genesis Chapter 4 -- The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 3r-3v | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_004_page_001.jpg, GEN_004_page_002.jpg
**Additional Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209) — LXX | **View Vaticanus online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209

---

**4:1** And the man knew Chavvah his wife, and she conceived and bore Qayin, and she said: "I have acquired a man with/[by means of] YHWH."

> Heb. וְהָאָדָם יָדַע אֶת-חַוָּה אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-קַיִן וַתֹּאמֶר קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת-יְהוָה
> *qaniti 'ish 'et-YHWH* — see Textual Notes on the preposition 'et.

**4:2** And she continued to bear his brother, Hevel. And Hevel became a keeper of the flock, and Qayin became a worker of the ground.

> Heb. וַתֹּסֶף לָלֶדֶת אֶת-אָחִיו אֶת-הָבֶל וַיְהִי-הֶבֶל רֹעֵה צֹאן וְקַיִן הָיָה עֹבֵד אֲדָמָה
> "Hevel" (הֶבֶל) means "breath/vapor/vanity" — the name carries anticipatory irony.

**4:3** And it came to pass at the end of days, and Qayin brought from the fruit of the ground an offering to YHWH.

> Heb. וַיְהִי מִקֵּץ יָמִים וַיָּבֵא קַיִן מִפְּרִי הָאֲדָמָה מִנְחָה לַיהוָה
> *miqqets yamim* — "at the end of days": the idiom implies "after a [set] period of time"; LXX: μεθ᾽ ἡμέρας ("after days").

**4:4** And Hevel, he also brought from the firstlings of his flock and from their fat-portions; and YHWH looked with-favor upon Hevel and upon his offering.

> Heb. וְהֶבֶל הֵבִיא גַם-הוּא מִבְּכֹרוֹת צֹאנוֹ וּמֵחֶלְבֵהֶן וַיִּשַׁע יְהוָה אֶל-הֶבֶל וְאֶל-מִנְחָתוֹ
> *vayyisha'* — "looked with-favor / had regard for": the Hiphil of שׁעה, meaning to gaze favorably upon. LXX: ἐπεῖδεν ("looked upon").

**4:5** But upon Qayin and upon his offering he did not look with-favor; and it burned-hot for Qayin greatly, and his face fell.

> Heb. וְאֶל-קַיִן וְאֶל-מִנְחָתוֹ לֹא שָׁעָה וַיִּחַר לְקַיִן מְאֹד וַיִּפְּלוּ פָּנָיו
> *vayyippelu fanav* — "his face(s) fell": Hebrew *panim* is plural in form; rendered literally "faces" in the original though referring to a single countenance. The falling of the face is an idiom for discouragement/anger.

**4:6** And YHWH said to Qayin: "Why has it burned-hot for you, and why have your face[s] fallen?

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-קַיִן לָמָּה חָרָה לָךְ וְלָמָּה נָפְלוּ פָנֶיךָ

**4:7** Is it not [thus]: if you do-well, [there is] lifting-up; and if you do not do-well, at the door sin crouches, and toward you [is] its desire, but you — you shall rule over it.

> Heb. הֲלוֹא אִם-תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל-בּוֹ
> See Textual Notes. This is among the most syntactically fractured verses in the Torah. *se'et* (שְׂאֵת) is an infinitive construct Qal of נשׂא meaning "lifting/being lifted up" — possibly: forgiveness of face, acceptance, or exaltation. The sentence has no explicit copula ("is") and the grammar is broken — preserved here as-is.

**4:8** And Qayin said to Hevel his brother; and it came to pass when they were in the field, and Qayin rose up against Hevel his brother and killed him.

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל-הֶבֶל אָחִיו וַיְהִי בִּהְיוֹתָם בַּשָּׂדֶה וַיָּקָם קַיִן אֶל-הֶבֶל אָחִיו וַיַּהַרְגֵהוּ
> The MT has *vayyomer Qayin el-Hevel* ("and Qayin said to Hevel") with no object — the speech is missing. The lacuna is ancient. See Textual Notes on the LXX and Samaritan Pentateuch additions.

**4:9** And YHWH said to Qayin: "Where [is] Hevel your brother?" And he said: "I do not know. Am I my brother's keeper?"

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל-קַיִן אֵי הֶבֶל אָחִיךָ וַיֹּאמֶר לֹא יָדַעְתִּי הֲשֹׁמֵר אָחִי אָנֹכִי
> *'ei* — "where?": a locative interrogative particle, archaic form.

**4:10** And he said: "What have you done? The voice of your brother's bloods is crying out to me from the ground.

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר מֶה עָשִׂיתָ קוֹל דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ צֹעֲקִים אֵלַי מִן-הָאֲדָמָה
> *demei* (דְּמֵי) — plural of "blood": "bloods." The plural is deliberate; the Rabbis understood it as meaning both Abel's blood and the blood of all his potential descendants. LXX: φωνὴ αἵματος ("voice of blood" — singular), which smooths the Hebrew plurality.

**4:11** And now, cursed are you from the ground, which has opened its mouth to receive the bloods of your brother from your hand.

> Heb. וְעַתָּה אָרוּר אַתָּה מִן-הָאֲדָמָה אֲשֶׁר פָּצְתָה אֶת-פִּיהָ לָקַחַת אֶת-דְּמֵי אָחִיךָ מִיָּדֶךָ

**4:12** When you work the ground, it shall not continue to give its strength to you; a fugitive and a wanderer you shall be on the earth.

> Heb. כִּי תַעֲבֹד אֶת-הָאֲדָמָה לֹא-תֹסֵף תֵּת-כֹּחָהּ לָךְ נָע וָנָד תִּהְיֶה בָּאָרֶץ
> *na' vanad* — "a fugitive and a wanderer": both words are Qal participles; the two words form a hendiadys conveying perpetual, rootless movement. LXX: στένων καὶ τρέμων ("groaning and trembling") — a notable divergence, likely reflecting a different Vorlage or a tendential interpretation.

**4:13** And Qayin said to YHWH: "My iniquity/punishment is too great to bear/be-forgiven."

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר קַיִן אֶל-יְהוָה גָּדוֹל עֲוֹנִי מִנְּשֹׂא
> *'avoni gadol mi-neso'* — See Textual Notes. The double ambiguity is preserved: (1) 'avon = iniquity OR punishment; (2) mi-neso' = "too great to be borne" OR "too great to be forgiven" (naso' = "to lift/carry" but idiomatically "to forgive"). The translation preserves both.

**4:14** See, you have driven me out today from the face of the ground, and from your face I shall be hidden; and I shall be a fugitive and a wanderer on the earth, and it shall come to pass that whoever finds me will kill me.

> Heb. הֵן גֵּרַשְׁתָּ אֹתִי הַיּוֹם מֵעַל פְּנֵי הָאֲדָמָה וּמִפָּנֶיךָ אֶסָּתֵר וְהָיִיתִי נָע וָנָד בָּאָרֶץ וְהָיָה כָל-מֹצְאִי יַהַרְגֵנִי
> *mi-phanekha 'essater* — "from your face I shall be hidden": the hiddenness from the divine presence is the deepest dimension of the curse.

**4:15** And YHWH said to him: "Therefore, whoever kills Qayin, sevenfold shall vengeance be taken on him." And YHWH set for Qayin a sign, so that whoever finds him should not strike him.

> Heb. וַיֹּאמֶר לוֹ יְהוָה לָכֵן כָּל-הֹרֵג קַיִן שִׁבְעָתַיִם יֻקָּם וַיָּשֶׂם יְהוָה לְקַיִן אוֹת לְבִלְתִּי הַכּוֹת-אֹתוֹ כָּל-מֹצְאוֹ
> *'ot* (אוֹת) — "sign": unspecified in the text; the nature of the mark is never described. LXX: σημεῖον ("sign/mark").

**4:16** And Qayin went out from the face of YHWH and he settled in the land of Nod, to the east of Eden.

> Heb. וַיֵּצֵא קַיִן מִלִּפְנֵי יְהוָה וַיֵּשֶׁב בְּאֶרֶץ-נוֹד קִדְמַת-עֵדֶן
> *Nod* (נוֹד) — from the same root as *nad* ("wanderer") in vv. 12, 14; the place-name embeds the curse. LXX: Ναιδ (transliteration). "East of Eden" echoes 3:24.

---

**4:17** And Qayin knew his wife and she conceived and bore Chanokh; and he [Qayin] was building a city and he called the name of the city by the name of his son: Chanokh.

> Heb. וַיֵּדַע קַיִן אֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתַּהַר וַתֵּלֶד אֶת-חֲנוֹךְ וַיְהִי בֹּנֶה עִיר וַיִּקְרָא שֵׁם הָעִיר כְּשֵׁם בְּנוֹ חֲנוֹךְ
> *Chanokh* (חֲנוֹךְ) — "dedicated/initiated." The same name as the seventh from Adam in the Sethite line (v. 17 vs. v. 17 and 5:18-24) — a notable textual correspondence.

**4:18** And to Chanokh was born Irad; and Irad fathered Mechuyael; and Mechuyael fathered Metushael; and Metushael fathered Lemekh.

> Heb. וַיִּוָּלֵד לַחֲנוֹךְ אֶת-עִירָד וְעִירָד יָלַד אֶת-מְחוּיָאֵל וּמְחִיּיָאֵל יָלַד אֶת-מְתוּשָׁאֵל וּמְתוּשָׁאֵל יָלַד אֶת-לָמֶךְ
> Note: the names Irad (עִירָד), Mechuyael (מְחוּיָאֵל), and Metushael (מְתוּשָׁאֵל) have obscure etymologies. LXX offers: Γαιδαδ, Μαιηλ, Μαθουσαλα — divergent transliterations.

**4:19** And Lemekh took for himself two wives; the name of the one [was] Adah, and the name of the second [was] Tzillah.

> Heb. וַיִּקַּח-לוֹ לֶמֶךְ שְׁתֵּי נָשִׁים שֵׁם הָאַחַת עָדָה וְשֵׁם הַשֵּׁנִית צִלָּה
> *Adah* (עָדָה) — "ornament/adornment." *Tzillah* (צִלָּה) — "shadow/shade." First explicit polygamy in the text.

**4:20** And Adah bore Yaval; he was the father of [those who] dwell in tents and [keep] herds.

> Heb. וַתֵּלֶד עָדָה אֶת-יָבָל הוּא הָיָה אֲבִי יֹשֵׁב אֹהֶל וּמִקְנֶה
> *'avi yoshev 'ohel u-miqneh* — "father of the one-who-dwells [in] tent and herd": the construct is a type-ancestor formula; Yaval is the ancestor-prototype of pastoral nomadic life.

**4:21** And his brother's name [was] Yuval; he was the father of all [those who] take-up the lyre and the pipe.

> Heb. וְשֵׁם אָחִיו יוּבָל הוּא הָיָה אֲבִי כָּל-תֹּפֵשׂ כִּנּוֹר וְעוּגָב
> *kinnor* (כִּנּוֹר) — the lyre/harp, a stringed instrument. *'ugav* (עוּגָב) — a pipe or wind instrument (precise identification uncertain; possibly a reed-flute or a primitive organ). LXX: κιθάρα καὶ ψαλτήριον.

**4:22** And Tzillah, she also bore Tuval-Qayin, the forger of every cutting-instrument of bronze and iron; and the sister of Tuval-Qayin [was] Naamah.

> Heb. וְצִלָּה גַם-הִוא יָלְדָה אֶת-תּוּבַל קַיִן לֹטֵשׁ כָּל-חֹרֵשׁ נְחֹשֶׁת וּבַרְזֶל וַאֲחוֹת תּוּבַל-קַיִן נַעֲמָה
> *lotesh kol-choresh* — "sharpener/forger of every cutting/working thing": *lotesh* = one who hammers/sharpens; *choresh* = artificer/craftsman or, literally, "one who cuts." The collocation points to the invention of metalworking (bronze = נְחֹשֶׁת, iron = בַּרְזֶל). *Naamah* (נַעֲמָה) — "pleasant/lovely."

---

**4:23** And Lemekh said to his wives:
   "Adah and Tzillah — hear my voice!
    Wives of Lemekh — give ear to my utterance!
    For a man I have killed for my wound,
    and a young-man for my bruise.

**4:24** For sevenfold Qayin shall be avenged,
    and Lemekh seventy and seven[fold]."

> Heb. (vv. 23-24)
> וַיֹּאמֶר לֶמֶךְ לְנָשָׁיו עָדָה וְצִלָּה שְׁמַעַן קוֹלִי נְשֵׁי לֶמֶךְ הַאְזֵנָּה אִמְרָתִי
> כִּי אִישׁ הָרַגְתִּי לְפִצְעִי וְיֶלֶד לְחַבֻּרָתִי
> כִּי שִׁבְעָתַיִם יֻקַּם-קָיִן וְלֶמֶךְ שִׁבְעִים וְשִׁבְעָה
>
> *Lamech's Song* — the oldest embedded poem in the narrative; characterized by synonymous parallelism in bicolon pairs. See Textual Notes for the interpretive crux of v. 23b.
>
> *le-fits'i / le-chaburati* — "for my wound / for my bruise": these are the preposition le- + a noun: the construction is ambiguous — it may mean "because of a wound dealt to me" (self-defense) or "at the cost of my own wound." The ambiguity is preserved literally.
>
> *yeled* (יֶלֶד) — "young man/boy/child" as parallel to *'ish* ("man") in the A-line; the parallelism suggests both terms refer to the same violent act, intensified.

---

**4:25** And the man knew his wife again, and she bore a son and called his name Shet, saying: "For Elohim has appointed for me another seed in place of Hevel, for Qayin killed him."

> Heb. וַיֵּדַע אָדָם עוֹד אֶת-אִשְׁתּוֹ וַתֵּלֶד בֵּן וַתִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ שֵׁת כִּי שָׁת-לִי אֱלֹהִים זֶרַע אַחֵר תַּחַת הֶבֶל כִּי הֲרָגוֹ קָיִן
> *Shet* (שֵׁת) — from the root שׁית "to place/appoint/set"; a paronomastic naming etymology. Note the divine name shift: here *Elohim* is used by Chavvah, whereas in v. 1 she used *YHWH* — though some MSS traditions vary. The Sethite line's beginning uses *Elohim*.

**4:26** And to Shet — to him also a son was born, and he called his name Enosh. Then [it was] begun [lit. it was profaned] to call on the name of YHWH.

> Heb. וּלְשֵׁת גַּם-הוּא יֻלַּד-בֵּן וַיִּקְרָא אֶת-שְׁמוֹ אֱנוֹשׁ אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא בְּשֵׁם יְהוָה
> *'Enosh* (אֱנוֹשׁ) — "mortal man/humanity" (cf. Ps. 8:5; 90:3); a name that stands in contrast to *'adam* (generic "man/humankind"), carrying connotations of human frailty and mortality.
> *'az huchal* — "then it was begun": the Hophal of חלל — see Textual Notes. This is one of the most debated final lines in Genesis 4.

---

## EMET's Textual Notes

**Note on 4:1 — qaniti 'ish 'et-YHWH (קָנִיתִי אִישׁ אֶת-יְהוָה)**

The crux is the preposition *'et* (אֶת). In Biblical Hebrew, *'et* serves two entirely distinct functions: (1) it is the direct-object marker (*ha-'et ha-yedi'ah*), and (2) it is the preposition meaning "with/together with." If *'et* here is the direct-object marker, Chavvah's declaration is theologically staggering — she would be claiming to have produced/acquired YHWH himself ("I have acquired a man: YHWH"), a reading that borders on identifying Cain with a theophany, or alternatively reflects a very primitive, pre-Mosaic conception of divinity. Most modern translations render it as "with the help of YHWH" (preposition), softening the claim. The LXX renders it διὰ τοῦ θεοῦ ("through God"), choosing a clear instrumental reading. The EMET translation preserves *both* readings by rendering "with/[by means of] YHWH." No Dead Sea Scrolls fragment of Gen 4:1 (4QGen-b) is sufficiently preserved to adjudicate this verse. The ambiguity is ancient and likely intentional.

**Note on 4:7 — the most broken syntax in Genesis**

MT: הֲלוֹא אִם-תֵּיטִיב שְׂאֵת וְאִם לֹא תֵיטִיב לַפֶּתַח חַטָּאת רֹבֵץ וְאֵלֶיךָ תְּשׁוּקָתוֹ וְאַתָּה תִּמְשָׁל-בּוֹ

The opening conditional clause "if you do well, *se'et*" lacks any main-clause verb; *se'et* (שְׂאֵת) is an infinitive absolute or construct functioning here as an implied noun: "lifting up / acceptance / exaltation." The grammar trails off without resolution. The second conditional clause ("if you do not do well") then pivots abruptly to a descriptive statement about sin crouching at the door, with the possessive pronoun *teshuqato* ("its desire") shifting gender — *chattat* ("sin") is grammatically feminine, but *teshuqato* ("its desire") bears a masculine suffix, a grammatical inconsistency that has generated extensive scholarly debate. Some attribute this to textual corruption; others argue it is a deliberate stylistic break. The word *teshuqah* (desire/longing) echoes Gen 3:16, connecting Eve's "desire" for Adam with sin's "desire" for Cain — a deliberate literary echo. The LXX renders the first clause as ἐὰν ὀρθῶς προσενέγκῃς, ὀρθῶς δὲ μὴ διέλῃς, ἥμαρτες ("if you offer rightly but do not divide rightly, you have sinned"), importing a cultic interpretation absent from the MT. The Peshitta and Vulgate align more closely with the MT. The EMET translation preserves the fractured syntax as-is; brackets indicate the supplied copula.

**Note on 4:8 — the missing speech**

The MT reads: *vayyomer Qayin el-Hevel achiv* ("and Qayin said to Hevel his brother") — and then nothing follows. The speech object is absent in the Masoretic Text. This is a well-known lacuna. The Samaritan Pentateuch, the LXX, the Peshitta, and the Vulgate all supply the missing words: "Let us go out to the field." (LXX: διέλθωμεν εἰς τὸ πεδίον.) This reading was likely original and accidentally omitted by haplography (the eye of a scribe jumping from *achiv* to *vayyehi*). The DSS fragment 4QGen-b does not cover this verse. The EMET translation preserves the MT lacuna without supplying the words, consistent with its strictly literal mandate, but this Note flags the near-certain original fuller text.

**Note on 4:10 — demei (bloods, plural)**

The Hebrew word *dam* ("blood") appears here in the plural construct form *demei* (דְּמֵי). Plural *damim* in Biblical Hebrew often carries the semantic weight of "bloodshed" or "life-blood poured out," but in this context the Talmudic tradition (Sanhedrin 37a) explicitly reads the plurality as encompassing Abel's potential descendants — "the blood of your brother and the blood of his descendants." The LXX (Codex Vaticanus confirmed) uses the singular αἵματος, smoothing the Hebrew. The EMET renders it "bloods" to preserve the plural.

**Note on 4:12 — na' vanad vs. LXX "groaning and trembling"**

MT *na' vanad* (נָע וָנָד): both words are active participles of synonymous roots meaning "to wander / to be driven." The LXX diverges markedly: στένων καὶ τρέμων ("groaning and trembling") — this has been explained as either a misreading of the Hebrew roots (confusing נוד with נאד, a root related to shaking/trembling, and נע with a root related to groaning) or as an interpretive Vorlage. The difference is significant theologically: the MT curse is geographical (Cain will wander the earth without a home), while the LXX renders it psychological/physiological (Cain will groan and tremble). The DSS 4QGen-b does not preserve this verse. The EMET follows MT.

**Note on 4:13 — 'avoni gadol mi-neso' (עֲוֹנִי גָּדוֹל מִנְּשֹׂא)**

This verse contains a double ambiguity that is impossible to resolve in translation without choosing one reading:

1. *'avon* (עָוֹן) means both "iniquity/sin" and "punishment/consequence-of-sin." Both senses are lexically attested. Here: "my sin is too great" OR "my punishment is too great."

2. *mi-neso'* is the preposition *min* + infinitive construct *naso'* (to lift/carry/bear/forgive). The phrase therefore means "too great from bearing" = "too great to be borne" (= too great a burden) OR "too great to be lifted up" = "too great to be forgiven" (since *naso' 'avon* idiomatically = "to forgive sin" throughout the Hebrew Bible; cf. Exod 34:7; Num 14:18).

The two ambiguities multiply: Cain could be saying (a) "My sin is too great to be forgiven," (b) "My punishment is too great to bear," (c) "My sin is too great to carry," or (d) "My punishment is too great to be forgiven." Reading (b) is the most natural in context (Cain is responding to the curse just pronounced), but reading (a) is equally defensible and is arguably the more theologically pregnant statement. The LXX renders: μείζων ἡ αἰτία μου τοῦ ἀφεθῆναί με ("my fault/cause is too great to be forgiven me") — choosing the guilt/forgiveness axis (reading 'a'). The EMET renders both nouns and both verbal possibilities as "/" constructions to preserve the full ambiguity.

**Note on 4:15 — the Sign of Cain**

The MT uses *'ot* (אוֹת), the same word used for the signs of the covenant (Gen 9:12-13) and the signs before Pharaoh (Exod 4:17). The word does not specify whether the sign is on Cain's body, or is some environmental sign set *for* Cain's benefit (i.e., as a warning to others). The Hebrew is: *vayyasem YHWH le-Qayin 'ot* — "and YHWH set/placed for Qayin a sign." The preposition *le-* ("for/to") governs Qayin, suggesting the sign is *for his protection*, not necessarily *on his person*. The LXX σημεῖον likewise does not specify. Speculation about the nature of the mark belongs to later interpretation, not the text itself.

**Note on 4:23-24 — Lamech's Song (Shirat Lamech)**

This is one of the oldest surviving poetic fragments in the Hebrew Bible. It exhibits classical Hebrew parallelism:

- Line 1a: *Adah ve-Tzillah shema'na qoli* — "Adah and Tzillah, hear my voice"
- Line 1b: *neshei Lemekh ha'azennah 'imrati* — "wives of Lemekh, give ear to my utterance"

Both *qol* ("voice") and *'imrah* ("utterance/word") are synonyms in the parallelism. The second bicolon:

- Line 2a: *ki 'ish haraqti le-fits'i* — "for a man I have killed for my wound"
- Line 2b: *ve-yeled le-chaburati* — "and a young-man for my bruise"

The poetic ambiguity of "for my wound" (*le-fits'i*) is central: does Lemekh kill *because* he was wounded (self-defense) or does he kill at great personal cost? Ancient interpreters divided sharply. The midrash (Gen. Rabbah 23:4) identifies the killed person as Cain himself, accidentally slain by the blind Lemekh guided by his son Tuval-Qayin — but this is post-biblical elaboration. The poem ends with a numerical intensification: sevenfold for Cain (v. 15) vs. seventy-sevenfold for Lemekh (v. 24), the hyperbolic logic of the blood-feud escalation. The LXX renders 77-fold as ἑβδομηκοντάκις ἑπτά, the same expression Jesus inverts in Mt 18:22 ("not seven times but seventy-seven times"), creating a direct intertextual counter to Lamech's song of vengeance.

**Note on 4:26 — 'az huchal liqro' (אָז הוּחַל לִקְרֹא)**

The Hophal of חלל (*huchal*) presents a classic crux. The standard reading is that *chalal* here is the verb חלל II ("to begin"), as in the common Hiphil *hechel* ("he began"). The form *huchal* is the Hophal passive: "it was begun." The translation "then it was begun to call on the name of YHWH" is the received reading (LXX: οὗτος ἤλπισεν ἐπικαλεῖσθαι τὸ ὄνομα κυρίου τοῦ θεοῦ — "this one hoped to call on the name of the LORD God," reading *huchal* as a form of יחל "to hope/wait"). However, the root חלל I means "to profane/defile." Several early interpreters (notably Maimonides in the Guide, and R. Eleazar in b. Shabbat 118a, though that text discusses different matters) were aware of this alternative: "then it was profaned to call on the name of YHWH," meaning prayer or invocation of YHWH began to be degraded in the time of Enosh. This reading — of cultic degradation in the antediluvian period — is attested in targumim (Targum Onqelos renders: "then the sons of men desisted from praying in the name of YHWH"), understanding the verse as the beginning of idolatry. The LXX unambiguously reads "hoped" (*ἤλπισεν*), presupposing a form of יחל rather than חלל. The MT's *huchal* from חלל is the harder reading and the EMET preserves the ambiguity by providing "begun [lit. it was profaned]" in translation.

**Note on DSS 4QGen-b (Dead Sea Scrolls)**

The fragmentary scroll 4QGen-b (Qumran Cave 4, Genesis fragment b) covers portions of Genesis 1-22 but with significant lacunae in Chapter 4. The extant fragments do not offer clean variants for the major cruxes in this chapter (4:1, 4:7, 4:8, 4:13, 4:26). Where 4QGen-b does overlap with Gen 4 text, it is substantially consonantal agreement with the proto-Masoretic tradition, consistent with the general pattern of the Qumran Genesis scrolls. No major divergences from the Leningrad Codex text are attested in the preserved portions of 4QGen-b for this chapter.

**Note on the divine name in Chapter 4**

Chapter 4 uses YHWH exclusively (without *Elohim*) from verse 1 through verse 16, and resumes with *Elohim* in verse 25. This pattern is consistent with the classical Documentary Hypothesis's assignment of alternating J (YHWH) and E/P (Elohim) sources. The J strand characterizes this narrative throughout Chapter 4. The return to *Elohim* in v. 25 marks the transition toward the genealogical material that will culminate in Chapter 5, itself characteristic of the P source. The EMET renders YHWH throughout as YHWH (unvocalized, per the tetragrammaton), resisting substitution with LORD, Adonai, or HaShem, as the source text carries the proper name without reduction.
